notes: Emergent social construct; Bandura's four efficacy channels require physical collaboration and shared professional identity.
AI dashboards can scaffold prediction and tracking; student must do the self-assessment act or mechanism hollows out.
ML can flag risk trajectories; teacher must synthesize with relational, contextual knowledge of individual students.
LLMs can decompose complex domains into learning progressions; expert teacher validation of accuracy is non-negotiable.
AI platforms can continuously monitor and flag tier transitions; targeted instruction must be physically delivered by trained humans.
Cognitive developmental progression is biologically fixed; AI can support stage-matched task design but cannot engineer the developmental shift.
Physical peer-to-peer teaching exchange is the causal mechanism; AI optimizing group composition touches only logistics.
Generative AI can identify misconceptions in student writing and serve contrasting texts; deep schema restructuring benefits from teacher mediation.
AI catalogs prior ability indicators; cannot alter baseline cognitive capacity.

influence: Strategy to integrate with prior knowledge
notes: AI pre-diagnostic tasks can map active schema; customized analogies require accuracy about what student actually knows.
AI adaptive micro-scaffolds can foster immediate confidence; durable self-efficacy is built through human encouragement over time.
Student perception of competence, trustworthiness, and goodwill is strictly interpersonal; AI fluency is not teacher credibility.
AI computer vision can map talk-time, questioning patterns, and engagement; human teacher must interpret and adjust practice.
AI generates near- and far-transfer problem sets; explicit modeling of cross-domain principles requires human instruction.
Socio-cognitive friction and shared community context require physical peer presence; AI can map participation patterns and flag gaps.
AI tutoring platforms can adapt hint specificity and difficulty in real-time; systematic fading required to prevent permanent dependency.
Ericsson-model deliberate practice requires expert diagnosis of specific errors; AI handles adaptive difficulty but not expert qualitative feedback.
Critical mechanism conflict: if AI summarizes for the student, student does not build the capacity. AI must evaluate student summaries, not produce them.
AI disengagement detection and micro-incentives support effort; sustained academic persistence mediated by teacher relationships.

influence: Interventions for students with learning needs
notes: AI-driven assistive software (TTS, interface adaptation) is legitimate; specialized physical and cognitive therapy requires trained human educators.
Procedural enough for full AI delivery; generative AI can produce personalized acronyms and retrieval associations at scale.
AI can structure prediction prompts; mechanism conflict if AI does the planning for the student rather than requiring student to plan.
AI real-time oral reading evaluation and word-level intervention is a legitimate AI-native implementation.
Generative AI assists in standardizing learning goals and logical sequences; classroom verbal delivery and active-listening checks remain human.
Mechanism conflict risk: AI must force student verbal elaboration, not produce elaboration on student's behalf.
AI prompt systems generate self-evaluation questions; deep metacognitive internalization of error requires teacher-guided think-alouds.
AI avatars can model predict/clarify/question/summarize; the peer-teaching social exchange requires physical human presence.
Procedural; AI flashcard and spacing platforms automate retrieval loops based on forgetting curve mathematics effectively.
AI can scaffold PD content delivery and resource curation; teacher learning through peer observation and coaching is human-dependent.
AI can reduce friction of seeking help (anonymous questions, peer matching); the interpersonal safety required depends on human classroom climate.
AI voice recognition assesses phonemic awareness effectively; structured human instruction in grapheme-phoneme mapping remains the pedagogical anchor.

Most-cited EdTech claim. Mechanism conflict: AI feedback can enable system-gaming (clicking hints for answers). One-third of feedback studies show negative effects. Vendor claims routinely strip this caveat.
Deep motivational orientation stems from interpersonal academic identity formation; AI gamification handles surface engagement only.
AI can design tasks that practice field-independent processing; cannot directly alter the underlying cognitive style.
AI can path gifted students through advanced curricula; human mentorship for advanced learners addresses social-emotional needs AI cannot.
AI onboarding engines can prompt explicit goal formulation; mechanism conflict if AI sets goals for student rather than requiring student to formulate them.
Rich problem-solving scenarios can be AI-generated; group facilitation and modeling of problem-solving heuristics requires human teacher.
Mechanism conflict: AI must evaluate student-produced outlines, not generate outlines for students.
AI can visualize conceptual hierarchies from student text; student must do the conceptual construction or mechanism is bypassed.
AI vocabulary engines deploying spaced retrieval and contextual sentence-building are a legitimate AI-native implementation.
AI provides diverse divergent-thinking prompts; subjective elaboration and collaborative creative evaluation require human facilitation.
Behavior modification requires physical presence, relational authority, and embodied social judgment; AI monitoring is peripheral.
AI can co-construct rubrics from historical baselines; student self-appraisal accuracy requires human calibration and validation.
AI blind grading can reduce expectation bias in assessment; teacher's ongoing cultivation of open-minded relationships is irreducibly human.

influence: Relations of high school to university achievement
notes: AI can map academic readiness indicators; human advising for individual transition decisions requires contextual judgment.
Critical mechanism conflict: perpetual AI Socratic prompting creates cognitive dependency. Without systematic fading, AI acts as external regulator, hollowing out independent metacognitive capacity.
AI can automate optimal spacing intervals; human teacher must ensure alignment to curriculum sequence and assess transfer.
Highly structured AI worked examples and practice items are legitimate; teacher orchestration of active-listening checks and live instruction is essential.
AI adaptive math practice platforms are a legitimate augmentation; conceptual modeling and misconception remediation require human teacher.
AI can calibrate task difficulty to zone of proximal development; mechanism conflict if AI sets goals rather than supporting student to set them.
Procedural; AI can deliver adaptive spelling retrieval with immediate corrective feedback at scale.
Embodied physical stimulation is the causal mechanism; AI has no role in the core intervention.
Mechanism conflict risk: AI must scaffold student self-monitoring, not monitor for the student.
Embodied civic action and community relationship-building cannot be replicated by AI; mechanism is entirely human and contextual.
AI may track working memory demands of tasks; cannot alter underlying working memory capacity.

influence: Full compared to pre-term/low birth weight
notes: Not a manipulable intervention; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Software-defined mastery often assesses procedural retrieval only. Deep conceptual transfer and self-pacing accountability require human oversight. Vendor claims of 100% mastery are unsupported.
AI can structure explicit strategy sequences; teacher delivery of modeling and guided practice is the human layer.
Strongest legitimate AI-native case in the dataset for specific learning needs; specialized human educator oversight still required.
AI disengagement detection triggers micro-interventions; sustained concentration and persistence require teacher relationship and task design.
AI tracks prior achievement indicators; cannot alter the underlying achievement baseline.
AI can adapt visual complexity of materials; clinical diagnosis and specialist intervention require human professionals.
Mechanism conflict: AI must require student to verbalize, not provide the verbalization for them.
Social interdependence theory requires genuine human peer collaboration; AI cannot replicate group accountability and shared social stakes.
Reasonable AI-native zone for procedural and content-delivery tasks in non-core subjects; teacher curriculum alignment required.
Mechanism conflict risk: immediate answer reveal after wrong response bypasses necessary retrieval effort. AI must require retrieval before providing correct answer.
Procedural; AI can embed adaptive questioning and branching within video content effectively.
AI can design structured re-attempt workflows; human teacher must maintain accountability for genuine re-engagement rather than procedural repetition.
AI can curate enrichment resources and adaptive challenge; depth of intellectual mentorship requires human expert relationship.
Social norm transmission, peer modeling, and belonging are embedded in physical peer community; AI cannot substitute.
Social-emotional teaching exchange, the act of one student teaching another, is the mechanism; AI monitoring of pairs is peripheral.
Social interdependence and group accountability require physical human presence; AI cannot replicate genuine cooperative stakes.
Embodied family relationships, warmth, stability, and shared routines are the mechanism; AI has no meaningful role in the causal chain.
AI cannot alter household income or socioeconomic barriers; at best AI can optimize resource allocation within existing constraints.
Empathetic, trusting interpersonal connection is the causal mechanism. AI automating grading can free teacher time for relationships; AI cannot be the relationship.
Mechanism conflict: perpetual AI scaffolding of self-regulation prevents student from internalizing the regulatory capacity.
Procedural logistics; AI can automate record-keeping and surface patterns, freeing teacher time for interpretation and action.
Embodied physical play, social negotiation, and intrinsic exploration cannot be replicated by AI-mediated interaction.
AI portals can streamline school-home communication; parental academic encouragement and home learning support are irreducibly human.

influence: Student rating of quality of teaching
notes: AI can systematize student feedback collection and surface patterns; teacher must interpret and act on feedback meaningfully.
Critical mechanism conflict: AI note-taking (transcription) removes the encoding process that generates the learning benefit. Student must produce the notes.
Mechanism conflict: the act of discriminating important from unimportant information is the learning process; AI doing it removes the benefit.
AI can detect off-task behavior; physical classroom environment and teacher authority manage sustained engagement.
AI science simulations and adaptive content delivery are legitimate; teacher facilitation of inquiry and experimental reasoning is human.
Broad institutional quality variable; AI can support administrative efficiency but does not address the underlying human institutional factors.
Mechanism conflict risk: AI must require student to articulate goals, not supply goals to the student.
AI can automate formative assessment collection and visualization; teacher must interpret patterns and adjust live instruction.
Socratic live questioning that adapts to student verbal responses in real-time requires human teacher; AI generates practice question sets.

Legitimate AI-native zone only with curriculum constraint, learner modeling, step-level feedback, and teacher oversight. Real-world compliance collapses without human enforcement. Vendor claims of Bloom's two-sigma solution are unsupported.
AI adaptive comprehension tasks are legitimate augmentation; teacher-facilitated meaning-making discussion is the human layer.
AI can surface cross-domain connections; teacher must design coherent integrated learning experiences.
Relational safety and peer accountability within small groups require physical co-presence; AI facilitation degrades the mechanism.
Dangerous misuse target: this aggregate pre-dates LLMs. Vendors citing this number for generative AI products are making an unsupported claim.
AI can frame tasks with real-world relevance connections; perception of genuine value is shaped by human teacher modeling and classroom culture.
Mechanism conflict: AI must scaffold practice of study skills, not execute study skills for the student.
Not manipulable; AI cannot alter a student's age relative to classmates.
High mechanism conflict risk: AI ghostwriting is the antithesis of the intervention. AI must evaluate and respond to student-produced writing, not produce writing for students.
Student-generated mental imagery is the mechanism; AI can prompt and guide the process but must not substitute images for the student.
Achievement motivation is shaped by interpersonal academic identity; AI gamification handles surface orientation only.
Early childhood development requires physical human care, warmth, and embodied interaction; AI has a limited support role only.
Social belonging, shared identity, and collective classroom culture require physical co-presence and human teacher cultivation.
AI generates diverse examples for pattern discovery; teacher facilitation of the inductive reasoning discussion is the human layer.
Legitimate AI-native zone for procedural content; developmental appropriateness and safeguarding require strong human teacher oversight.
AI can dramatically increase reading exposure through adaptive text selection; teacher modeling of reading as a valued activity shapes motivation.
Outdoor challenge, physical risk management, and group resilience-building are entirely embodied; AI has no meaningful role.
Structural variable; AI cannot alter physical school size or the relational dynamics it creates.
AI blind-grading and pattern detection can surface implicit low expectations; teacher must actively recalibrate their beliefs and behaviors.
Community of inquiry requires human facilitation of genuine philosophical dialogue; AI can supply texts and seed questions.
AI can structure communication skill practice tasks; authentic communication competency requires real human interaction.
Intrinsic motivation is shaped by autonomy, competence, and relatedness (SDT); AI supports competence feedback but relatedness requires human.
AI low-stakes practice reduces performance anxiety around tasks; clinical anxiety management requires human therapeutic relationship.
Mechanism conflict: AI must require student to generate explanations, not provide explanations to the student.
AI can generate visual advance organizers; teacher must connect them explicitly to live instructional sequence.
Critical mechanism conflict: AI writing tools that produce text for students are the antithesis of this intervention. Legitimate only as feedback and editing tools on student-produced writing.
Legitimate AI-native zone for procedural and content delivery; mechanism conflict risk high in writing and problem-solving tasks.
Positive academic self-concept is built through mastery experiences validated by trusted adults; AI supports mastery experience design.
AI can curate and deliver PD content; peer observation, coaching relationships, and collaborative reflection are irreducibly human.
AI can structure tasks connecting creative and academic domains; teacher evaluation of creative quality shapes student perception of worth.
Mechanism conflict risk: AI must support student commitment to self-set goals, not set goals for students.
Positive interdependence, individual accountability within groups, and face-to-face interaction are the mechanisms; AI cannot replicate.
AI can surface resources and scaffold inquiry structure; genuine inquiry reasoning and question-formation require human facilitation.
After-school program benefits are partly relational and community-based; AI content delivery is peripheral to the human relationship component.
Social skill acquisition requires embodied human interaction, real-time social feedback, and practice in genuine social contexts.

influence: Relations of high school achievement to career performance
notes: Correlation between school and career performance; AI career mapping tools are adjacent but do not address the underlying mechanism.
Arts learning benefits from embodied practice, human performance, and audience response; AI tools are peripheral.
AI can provide career information and mapping; human mentorship, networking, and personal advising are the primary career intervention mechanisms.
Music learning requires embodied physical practice, human performance feedback, and ensemble social experience.
Procedural; AI can generate infinite correctly-worked examples aligned to student error patterns and curriculum level.
Below hinge as educational tool; physical phone presence introduces cognitive load and off-task multitasking. Enforcement of bans requires human authority.
AI translation and language practice tools support bilingual instruction; human cultural competency and linguistic modeling are the primary mechanism.
Student-centered teaching requires teacher real-time responsiveness to student interests and needs; AI personalizes content selection peripherally.
Content attitude is primarily shaped by teacher modeling of enthusiasm and relevance; AI framing is peripheral.
Therapeutic alliance is the primary mechanism of counseling effectiveness; AI chatbot substitution degrades genuine therapeutic relationship.
Behavioral authority, physical presence, and relational credibility are the mechanisms of effective classroom management; AI has no meaningful role.
AI can enhance simulation fidelity and adaptivity; teacher must explicitly connect simulation learning to curriculum and ensure transfer.
AI chess engines provide unlimited practice opponents; human instruction in strategic thinking and competitive motivation are the primary mechanism.
Character and motivation programs work through human modeling, relational accountability, and school culture; AI content delivery is peripheral.
De-escalation of disruptive behavior requires physical presence, relational authority, and embodied social judgment; AI monitoring is peripheral.
Genuine collaboration requires physical co-presence, real-time social negotiation, and shared accountability; AI-mediated collaboration degrades the mechanism.
Creative thinking development requires human facilitation of evaluation, refinement, and social validation of creative output.
Reducing stereotype threat requires human relational affirmation and belonging signals; AI has no meaningful role in the core mechanism.
Legitimate AI-native zone for procedural math practice; effect size below hinge means AI math tools should not substitute for high-effect human instruction.
Medical treatment; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Instructional leadership requires relational authority, contextual judgment, and embodied presence; AI administrative tools are peripheral.
School climate is built through human relationships, shared norms, and leadership behavior; AI climate surveys are data-collection tools only.
Aggregate variable; individual teacher quality improvement requires human coaching, not AI.
AI can generate summaries, glossaries, and study guides; effect size below hinge suggests these are insufficient as primary interventions.
External accountability is structural and political; AI reporting tools are administrative supports only.
Learning styles as a construct has weak empirical support; AI personalization should be based on prior knowledge and performance, not style matching.
Embodied physical manipulation of objects is the causal mechanism; virtual AI manipulatives have reduced physical encoding benefit.
Structural grouping decisions require human judgment about equity and individual student needs; AI can support logistics only.
AI test preparation tools are legitimate but address a below-hinge intervention; should not substitute for high-effect instructional investments.
Legitimate AI-native zone for procedural content at high school level; 0.30 effect size means AI tools should augment, not replace, high-effect human instruction.
Mindfulness practice benefits from human facilitation and relational context; AI apps provide scripts but not the relational embedding.
Physical presence in the home, relational trust between visitor and family, and embodied support are the mechanism; AI has no meaningful role.
Clinical CBT effectiveness depends on therapeutic alliance; AI tools may support between-session practice but cannot replace human therapeutic relationship.
Legitimate AI-native zone; 0.29 effect size is the honest number vendors selling AI digital tools should be required to cite.
Legitimate AI-native zone for reading technology; below hinge effect size means AI reading tools should not be primary literacy intervention.

Critical mechanism conflict: homework works through independent practice and consolidation; AI assistance that provides answers bypasses the consolidation process entirely. High vendor activity makes this a dangerous zone.
Structural policy; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Early childhood development requires physical human care and embodied play; AI tools are peripheral to the primary developmental mechanism.
School improvement requires human leadership, collective professional development, and organizational culture change; AI data analytics are a support tool.
Calculator use is appropriate after conceptual mastery; mechanism conflict if used to bypass the development of numerical reasoning.
Inclusion requires human specialist support, relational integration, and physical co-presence; AI logistics tools are peripheral.
Personality traits are stable; AI cannot alter underlying student personality attributes.
Physical exercise is the causal mechanism; AI wearables track but do not deliver the intervention.
Student health is a background variable; AI health monitoring is peripheral to the educational mechanism.
Embodied real-world experiences are the mechanism; AI can facilitate discovery and access but cannot substitute for the experiences themselves.
The relational commitment of a volunteer tutor is part of the mechanism; AI substitution removes the human relationship element.
Problem-based learning requires human facilitation of authentic problem-solving; AI resource scaffolding is peripheral.
AI presentation tools are legitimate; 0.26 effect size means AI-generated slides should not be promoted as a significant learning intervention.
Growth mindset development requires human modeling of effort and persistence; AI prompts are a peripheral scaffold.
Background variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Institutional and community context variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Competitive motivation is primarily social; AI leaderboards are a weak substitute for genuine peer competition.
Background variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Summer learning loss prevention requires sustained engagement; AI content delivery is peripheral to the relational and motivational challenge.
Teacher personality traits are stable background variables; AI cannot alter teacher personality.
AI personalization technically delivers individualized instruction; below-hinge effect size suggests individualization alone is insufficient without high-effect pedagogical strategies.
AI is the technological successor to programmed instruction; below-hinge effect size is the honest number for this intervention class.
Legitimate AI-native zone for science simulations and data tools; below-hinge effect size means AI science tools should not substitute for high-effect human instruction.
Teacher verbal modeling of precise, sophisticated language use is an embodied human mechanism; AI cannot replicate genuine verbal ability in a teacher.
AI polling and response systems are the natural successor to clickers; below-hinge effect size is the honest number.
AI can generate visual and multimedia content; teacher-facilitated interpretation and meaning-making is the primary mechanism.
School funding is a structural policy variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Structural variable; AI tools do not change the physical teacher-to-student ratio that drives the mechanism.
AI can schedule interleaved practice sequences; below-hinge effect size means interleaving alone is an insufficient primary strategy.
Genuine discovery learning requires human facilitation of inquiry; AI resource provision is peripheral.
Physical small group interaction is the mechanism; technology introduction often degrades face-to-face collaboration rather than enhancing it.
College student support works through human advising, belonging, and relational accountability; AI tools are logistical supports.
Extra-curricular benefits come from embodied participation, skill development, and human mentorship; AI logistics tools are peripheral.
Paternal engagement is a relational family variable; AI has no meaningful role in the causal mechanism.
Aptitude-treatment matching requires complex human judgment about individual learner profiles; AI personalization is a weak proxy.
AI can generate prerequisite learning maps; teacher expertise in curriculum sequencing is the primary mechanism.
Team teaching benefits from human professional collaboration and complementary expertise; AI coordination tools are logistical supports.
Flexible grouping requires human judgment about social dynamics and equity; AI composition optimization is a peripheral support.
AI web-based learning platforms are a legitimate use case; 0.18 is the honest effect size baseline vendors should be required to cite.
Student stress levels are shaped by home, relational, and institutional factors; AI has no meaningful causal role.
Background family variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
AI devices are a natural extension of one-on-one laptop programs; 0.16 is the honest effect size baseline. Vendors claiming transformative impact from device deployment are making unsupported claims.
Home schooling effectiveness depends on family relationship quality and curriculum coherence; AI content tools are logistical supports.
Legitimate peripheral AI use case; below-hinge effect size limits claim strength significantly.
Parental autonomy-supportive parenting requires human relational warmth and responsiveness; AI tools cannot replicate this dynamic.
AI dramatically improves distance education delivery; 0.13 effect size is a warning that distance education itself is a low-effect modality regardless of AI enhancement.
Chronobiological variable; AI scheduling tools are peripheral to the biological mechanism.
Ethnic identity development requires human cultural community and relational modeling; AI has no meaningful role.
Juvenile delinquent program effectiveness depends on human mentorship and relational accountability; AI logistics tools are peripheral.
School choice is a structural policy variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Structural tracking has documented equity harms; AI should support flexible, responsive grouping rather than rigid tracking.
Mentoring effectiveness depends on authentic human relationship, mutual commitment, and genuine care; AI cannot replicate this.
Initial teacher training requires human coaching, observation, and feedback on real teaching practice; AI content tools are logistical supports.
AI can generate diverse assessment formats; below-negligible effect size suggests test format variation alone has minimal impact.
Teacher's pedagogical content knowledge — knowing how to teach a specific subject — requires human expertise development; AI cannot substitute.
Structural demographic variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Negligible effect size; AI music selection tools are a peripheral distraction from high-effect instructional investments.
Diversity education requires human facilitation of genuine cross-cultural dialogue; AI content delivery is peripheral.
Institutional structure variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Negligible effect size; AI timetabling tools are administrative efficiencies with minimal educational impact.
Structural policy decision; AI flexible grouping tools support implementation but do not drive the educational mechanism.
Demographic variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Embodied physical movement is the mechanism; AI has no meaningful role.
Institutional structure variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Middle school transition support depends on human relational continuity and advising; AI logistics tools are peripheral.
Effect size at negligible band; AI mastery goal tracking is a peripheral support that does not warrant significant investment.
Negligible effect size compared to phonics instruction (0.70); AI whole language tools should not be prioritized over phonics-based interventions.
Living context variable; AI has no meaningful role in the educational mechanism.
Performance pay is a structural policy variable with negligible effect size; AI measurement tools do not address the underlying motivation mechanism.
Biological variable entirely outside educational AI scope.
Negligible effect size; multi-grade AI differentiation tools should not be prioritized over high-effect instructional investments.
Teacher humor is relational and contextually embedded; AI-generated humor is artificial and pedagogically irrelevant at this effect size.
Background variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
Negligible effect size; student autonomy in learning is important pedagogically but effect size suggests it does not drive achievement gains alone.
Background demographic variable; AI has no role.
Physical classroom structure variable; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
AI is the natural delivery medium for distance education; 0.01 effect size is a systemic warning that distance education modality itself limits achievement regardless of technology quality.
Performance goals (ego-oriented, not mastery-oriented) harm achievement; AI scaffolding of performance goals perpetuates the harm.
Summer learning loss is a structural calendar variable; AI summer learning platforms partially offset loss but do not address root cause.
Sleep is a biological variable; AI sleep tracking is peripheral to the educational mechanism.
Mechanism conflict: AI gamification systems risk reinforcing surface (extrinsic reward) motivation orientation, potentially deepening the harm.
Socioeconomic barrier; AI has no role in the causal mechanism. Structural poverty requires structural intervention.
Family disruption variable; AI has no meaningful role in addressing the underlying harm.
Screen time harm is mediated by displacement of learning time and sleep; AI monitoring tools require human authority for enforcement.
Students feeling disliked requires human relational repair; AI chatbot substitution for human social connection risks deepening the harm.
Exclusionary discipline has documented harm; restorative alternatives require human relational judgment and community repair processes.
AI language feedback tools trained on standard dialect corpora risk perpetuating linguistic discrimination; requires careful auditing for dialect bias.
Retention harms achievement; AI predictive analytics identifying retention candidates should prompt targeted intervention, not retention decisions.
Child welfare variable requiring human institutional and legal intervention; AI has no role in the causal mechanism.
School mobility harm is primarily relational disruption; AI transition scaffolding tools partially mitigate logistics but do not restore relational continuity.
Clinical depression requires human therapeutic intervention; AI mental health tools are peripheral supports only.
AI adaptive difficulty can reduce boredom from under-challenge; mechanism conflict risk if gamification substitutes for genuine intellectual challenge.
AI captioning, sign language tools, and assistive technology are a genuinely important augmentation; specialist human deaf education remains essential.

AI interface simplification and pacing tools are legitimate supports; cannot address the neurobiological basis of ADHD. The negative effect size reflects ADHD's impact on achievement, not an intervention effect.
